main_summary.summary: Charles "Chuck" Siler, interviewed by David Cline on May 10, 2013, at the African American Museum in Fair Park, Dallas, recounts a civil rights life rooted in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. Born into a family of independent business owners, World War II veterans, and Methodist church musicians, Siler describes his earliest protest as quitting the Boy Scouts after Black scouts were placed behind horses in the parade. He participated in the 1960 McKinley High School picket of the segregated state library, was arrested in December 1961 during the Southern University Bon Marché Center demonstrations, and spent three weeks in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail while police tear-gassed protesters singing "We Shall Overcome" outside. Expelled from Southern, Siler ran an NAACP voter registration project for Rev. T.J. Jemison, met Martin Luther King Jr. at the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta, and was a defendant in a federal injunction case against CORE that drew Gloster Current as counsel. He served as a U.S. Army information officer in Vietnam (1967-68) writing hometown news releases featuring Black soldiers, then worked at Southern University athletics, Black Associated Sports Enterprise, the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under Edwin Edwards, and as curator at the Louisiana State Museum, where he produced a 1994 exhibit on Mardi Gras Indians.
key_themes: Black student protest at Southern University
Vietnam military service and Black soldiers
main_summary.historical_significance:
Siler's account is one of the most detailed extant first-person testimonies of the December 1961 Baton Rouge sit-in movement and the mass-arrest event that produced the federal Garner v. Louisiana-era case law. He provides specific identifying detail on the Southern University expellees (Marvin Robinson, Donald Moss, Major Johns), the CORE legal team out of New Orleans (Lolis Elie, Robert F. Collins, Nils R. Douglas), and the local NAACP leadership (Rev. T.J. Jemison, Rev. Robert Walker, Dr. Dupuy Anderson, Pearl George) that the broader literature on the Louisiana movement underrepresents.

The interview also documents the underdiscussed history of racial conflict among American troops in Vietnam, Siler's role producing Black-soldier hometown news features for First Logistical Command at Qui Nhon, and the founding of the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under Governor Edwin Edwards — the first state-level division of Black culture in the United States. His later curatorial work and editorial cartooning for the Louisiana Weekly extends the testimony from movement participant to cultural-institution builder.

chapters.title: Baton Rouge Family and Early Influences
chapters.summary: Siler describes his upbringing in Baton Rouge by a beautician mother, piano-teacher grandmother, and railroad-janitor grandfather who rode a bicycle to work to avoid paying segregated bus fare. He details a family of six maternal uncles — several World War II and Korean War veterans — who ran independent businesses including a Lafayette cleaners, tailoring shop, insurance sales, and catering. His grandmother encouraged his early drawing; his father played baseball with Satchel Paige and started a Compton, California Black baseball league.

chapters.title: First Protests at McKinley High
chapters.summary: Siler recounts his first protest — quitting the Boy Scouts as a 12-year-old after Black scouts were placed behind horses in the Baton Rouge parade, then forming an explorer post at his school. At McKinley High School in spring 1960, he and classmates including Moses Edwards, Betty Jones, and E. Nola Price organized a picket of the segregated state library after final examinations. The Southern University demonstration of March 1960 had locked students on campus to prevent them joining.

chapters.title: Family Models of Standing Up
chapters.summary: Siler describes how his uncles modeled resistance to white violence: his uncle Willie Jr., shot by another American soldier and carrying shrapnel until his death; his uncle Gene, who ran the first Black cleaners in Lafayette and kept guns hidden throughout his property; his uncle Ralph, who beat an assailant in an Army barracks and returned to his prayers. The grandfather rode a bicycle to the railroad rather than ride a segregated bus.

chapters.title: Southern University Sit-ins and Jail
chapters.summary: As a sophomore art major at Southern University in 1961, Siler trained in nonviolent protest under CORE's Dave Dennis Sr. and Jerome Smith and was arrested December 12, 1961 picketing the Bon Marché Center with Willie Bradford and Thomas Peete. He spent the Christmas holidays in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail, where Baton Rouge police tear-gassed an estimated 5,000 protesters who had assembled outside singing "We Shall Overcome." Lolis Elie, Robert F. Collins, and Nils R. Douglas served as defense counsel.

chapters.title: Voter Registration and NAACP Atlanta Convention
chapters.summary: Expelled from Southern after the December 1961 arrests, Siler was hired by Rev. T.J. Jemison and Rev. Robert Walker to run an NAACP voter registration project in South Baton Rouge, walking door-to-door to teach residents to pass voter registration forms. At the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta, he confronted a Klan picket line, joined a sit-in at the Krystal restaurant with Al Jones, met Medgar Evers three days before his June 1963 assassination, and ran for the NAACP Youth Commission. Clara Luper, Daisy Bates, and Ella Baker mentored him.

chapters.title: Federal Injunction and Return to Southern
chapters.summary: In fall 1962 Siler took children to Fuqua elementary school and was threatened with prosecution for violating a federal injunction directed at CORE. Rev. Jemison brought in NAACP attorney Gloster Current, who negotiated with East Baton Rouge District Attorney Sargent Pitcher; the case did not proceed. Siler returned to Southern, became newspaper editor and a cartoonist for the student paper, and graduated in January 1966.

chapters.title: California, the Draft, and Vietnam
chapters.summary: Siler moved briefly to California to study at Frank Silvera's American Theatre, worked with the Hollywood NAACP, and was drafted in 1967. After nuclear weapons school and light vehicle driver training, he was assigned at Fort Huachuca, Arizona, where he was on charge of quarters the night Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated and worked to defuse tensions among Black soldiers. He deployed to Vietnam as a senior clerk in an AG section at Qui Nhon.

chapters.title: Vietnam Information Office and Black Soldiers
chapters.summary: At First Logistical Command's information office at Qui Nhon, Siler wrote hometown news releases on Black soldiers — work that previously accounted for only about five percent of the unit's output. He documented an orphanage where Vietnamese children had lost limbs and recounts witnessing race-related fights between Black and white American troops in Vietnam cities. He worked with Major (later General) Sherian Cadoria, a fellow Southern University alumna.
related_events: Race riots among U.S. troops in Vietnam

chapters.title: Southern Sports, Black Culture, and the State Museum
chapters.summary: After Vietnam, Siler became sports information director at Southern University, working with Mel Blount and Harold Carmichael. He joined Black Associated Sports Enterprise, an early attempt at a Black sports television network that preceded BET, and proposed films on Robert Smalls. Returning to Louisiana, he served at the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under director Ruth Wesley — the first state-level Black culture division, created by Governor Edwin Edwards. He then joined the Louisiana State Museum, where his 1994 Mardi Gras Indians exhibit drew on African-American photographers.
related_events: Louisiana Office of Black Culture founding

chapters.title: Culture, Politics, and Teaching
chapters.summary: Siler reflects on the inseparability of politics and culture and his work teaching children about jazz, the blues, and Mardi Gras Indian beadwork at museum workshops. He discusses being an editorial cartoonist for the Louisiana Weekly and the Black Commentator, his friendships with comics including Sally Marr at the Comedy Store and Improv in the 1970s and 1980s, and his self-definition as an "American African."

chapters.title: Mentors, the Pen, and Mrs. Williams's Vote
chapters.summary: Siler credits Southern University humanities professor Huel Perkins, who closed his classes reciting Cyrano de Bergerac, and art faculty Gene Paul Hubbard, Frank Hayden, and Harold Cureau as formative mentors. He describes accepting the Library of Congress and NMAAHC invitation after the birth of his grandson Ethan, so the family record would survive. He closes with the story of Mrs. Williams, an 84-year-old Baton Rouge woman who succeeded on her third attempt to register to vote in 1962; Siler carried a hidden gun in case of attack.
related_events: John Hope Franklin and Lonnie Bunch interview invitation

chapters.title: Faith, Dialogue, and Closing Reflection
chapters.summary: Siler discusses his independent political stance, his friendships with a Black Republican, a Libertarian media operative, and two Muslim imams, and his appreciation for the American Friends Service Committee, which provided meeting space to the civil rights movement. He criticizes Black ministers who refused to let voter-registration workshops meet in their churches out of fear and concludes that artists' motivation comes from the audience, not money.
related_events: American Friends Service Committee civil rights support
